source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 9.6.12 orphan and zombie (pp. 170-171)

options: A child whose parent has died; init adopts it and reaps it when it finishes
A process that never had a parent
A terminated process still in the process table
A process with no memory allocated
questions.why: When a parent dies, its children become orphans and are adopted by init, which collects their status until they end. Orphans are harmless.

options: A child that has exited but whose parent never called wait()/waitpid() to reap its status
A process running without a terminal
A process that has been killed with SIGKILL
questions.why: A zombie has finished but its parent hasn't retrieved its exit status, so it lingers in the process table.

questions.q: Why are zombie processes a problem?
options: They occupy slots/resources in the process table, degrading performance and blocking creation of new processes
They are actually harmless, unlike orphans
questions.why: Zombies hold entries in the process table; enough of them can hurt performance and prevent new processes being created.

questions.q: In ps output, which state code marks a zombie (defunct) process?
questions.why: Z is a defunct (zombie) process; R is running/runnable, S interruptible sleep, D uninterruptible sleep, T stopped.

questions.q: What does the ps state code 'D' mean?
questions.why: D is uninterruptible sleep (typically I/O); the 'dead' state is X, which should never be seen.
